text: The surgeon begins the dissection by accessing the lesser sac, taking down the gastrocolic ligament. The dissection continues along the greater curvature of the stomach until reaching the upper pole of the spleen, exposing a very large splenic mass.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and a vessel sealer device to detach attachments to the splenic lesion. The splenocolic ligament is dissected from medial to lateral, followed by the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The robotic third arm provides medial retraction of the colon, allowing for enhanced articulation and dexterity of the robotic instruments during lung dissection in challenging areas.

The surgeon continues medial dissection along the gastrocodic ligaments using a vessel sealer. The large size of the splenic lesion visibly displaces surrounding tissues, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon uses robotic shears to dissect and isolate the splenic vein and artery, following the impression of the splenic artery. Multiple branches of the vessels are encountered during the procedure.
The surgeon injects endocyanin green intravenously and activates the firefly function of the robotic system, illuminating the vessels supplying the spleen at the hilum for clear identification.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the isolated and clipped splenic vessels, completing the dissection and isolation process.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the spleen by taking down the splenophrenic ligament. An extra large endocatch bag is used to contain the spleen, which is then extracted through a previous C-section incision.
